data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Shell (LSE:SHEL) After Its 38% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Shell's current share price still offers value after a strong run, this breakdown will help you see what the numbers are really saying.
  • Over the last year, Shell has recorded a 38.2% return, with 28.4% year to date, 13.8% over the past month, and 2.0% over the last week. This naturally raises questions about how much upside or risk is now priced in.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Shell's positioning in the global energy market and its capital allocation priorities. These are themes that often influence how investors think about long term cash generation. These headlines provide useful context when weighing whether current returns align with the underlying value of the business.
  • Shell currently has a valuation score of 4/6, indicating it screens as undervalued on several checks. The sections ahead will compare different valuation approaches before finishing with a broader framework that can help you judge value more confidently.

Shell delivered 38.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Oil and Gas industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Shell Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model looks at the cash Shell is expected to generate in the future, then discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the whole business could be worth right now.

Shell’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$23.9b. Using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, analysts and Simply Wall St projections extend that figure out over the next decade, with forecast free cash flow of US$29.5b in 2030. Estimates for the first few years are based on analyst forecasts, and later years are extrapolated by Simply Wall St rather than coming directly from analyst models.

On this basis, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$86.10 per share. Compared with the current share price, the model implies the stock trades at a 58.8% discount, which indicates Shell screens as materially undervalued on this cash flow view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Shell is undervalued by 58.8%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 4 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Shell.

Approach 2: Shell Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful gauge because it links what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. It tends to reflect how the market weighs a company’s earnings power relative to others.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends a lot on growth expectations and risk. Higher growth or more resilient earnings can support a higher multiple, while more uncertainty or weaker profitability usually lines up with a lower one.

Shell trades on a P/E of 14.8x. That sits close to the Oil and Gas industry average of 15.3x and below the wider peer average of 20.0x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Shell is 24.3x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that could make sense for the company given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk profile. It is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry, which may not share the same fundamentals.

Comparing the current 14.8x P/E with the Fair Ratio of 24.3x suggests the shares screen as undervalued on this earnings multiple view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 5 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Shell Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St this comes through Narratives, which let you set out a clear story for Shell, link that story to explicit forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins, and then translate those assumptions into a Fair Value you can compare with today’s share price on the Community page used by millions of investors.

Instead of only relying on a single DCF or P/E snapshot, a Narrative captures your view of Shell’s LNG position, capital discipline and cash returns, or any concerns you may have about chemicals, energy transition or buybacks. It then ties that view to numbers that update when fresh news or earnings arrive, so you can quickly see if the gap between Fair Value and Price still supports holding, adding or trimming.

For example, one Shell Narrative currently anchors on a Fair Value of about £27.53, closer to the lower analyst price target, while another leans toward £34.65, nearer the consensus target. By seeing both side by side you can decide which story fits your expectations and whether the current price of around £34.44 feels high, low or broadly in line with your own view.

For Shell, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Shell Narratives:

  • Capital discipline theme that points to the Value over Volume approach and regular share buybacks as important for per share value creation.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what Korn Ferry's future cash generation is worth in today's dollars.

For Korn Ferry, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is reported at about $305.3 million. Based on analyst inputs for the near term and Simply Wall St extrapolations after that, projected Free Cash Flow for 2035 is $394.2 million, with intermediate yearly projections between 2026 and 2034 ranging from $308.0 million to $381.6 million. These projections are all in $ and are modeled using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach.

Rolling all of those cash flows into the DCF model results in an estimated intrinsic value of $133.17 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $63.36, the model implies the stock is about 52.4% undervalued on this cash flow view.

Approach 2: Korn Ferry Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to connect what you pay per share with the earnings that support that price. It gives a quick sense of how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower multiple.

Korn Ferry currently trades on a P/E of 12.39x. This sits below both the Professional Services industry average P/E of 18.98x and a peer group average of 17.23x. The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for these business characteristics rather than assuming all companies deserve the same multiple. data.title: Beijing Beida Jade Bird Universal Sci Tech (SEHK:8095) Half Year Loss Tests Turnaround Narrative
data.description:
Beijing Beida Jade Bird Universal Sci-Tech (SEHK:8095) has reported FY 2025 first half revenue of CN¥300.6 million with basic EPS of a CN¥0.0187 loss, setting the tone for how margins are holding up after the recent return to profit over the last 12 months. The company has seen revenue range from CN¥214.8 million in 1H 2024 to CN¥284.1 million in 2H 2024 and then CN¥300.6 million in 1H 2025. Basic EPS shifted from a CN¥0.0040 loss in 1H 2024 to CN¥0.0696 in 2H 2024 before the latest half year loss, putting the focus squarely on how consistently the business can now defend its margins.

See our full analysis for Beijing Beida Jade Bird Universal Sci-Tech.

Advertisement

TTM profit of CN¥77m despite 1H loss

  • Across the last 12 months, net income excluding extra items was CN¥76.982 million on CN¥584.693 million of revenue, even though 1H 2025 on its own showed a net loss of CN¥28.372 million on CN¥300.562 million of revenue.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that trailing 12 month basic EPS of CN¥0.050831 remains positive after earlier losses. However, it now sits alongside a fresh half year loss and a CN¥38.3 million one off charge, so optimism about a sustained turnaround has to account for this mix of profit over the year and pressure in the most recent period.
    • Supporters pointing to the CN¥99.305 million net income in 2H 2024 and CN¥105.354 million net income for that half can reference a clear profitable stretch, but the swing back to a CN¥28.372 million loss in 1H 2025 signals that earnings have not settled into a steady pattern yet.
    • The five year record of earnings declining at about 4.8% per year also sits in the background, so the recent 12 month profit is just one data point in a longer series of ups and downs rather than a clear new phase.

Premium 16.2x P/E against peers

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 16.2x, compared with a peer average of 10.5x and an Asian Industrials industry average of 12.5x, so the market is paying a higher multiple for Beijing Beida Jade Bird Universal Sci-Tech at a current share price of HK$0.94.
  • Critics highlight that this premium multiple sits alongside a five year earnings trend that declined by about 4.8% per year and trailing results that include a CN¥38.3 million one off loss. This means the valuation is higher than peers even though the long term earnings record and recent adjustments do not obviously point to stronger performance.
    • The return to profitability over the last 12 months, with CN¥76.982 million of net income excluding extra items, helps explain why some investors might accept a higher P/E, yet it does not erase the multi year decline in earnings.
    • Because there are no provided forecasts for revenue or earnings relative to the Hong Kong market, this premium is being set without clear forward growth benchmarks, so holders need to judge whether the recent profit and business mix justify paying above peer and industry P/E levels.

One off CN¥38.3m loss distorts trend

  • The trailing 12 month figures include a CN¥38.3 million one off loss, which affects how cleanly investors can read the CN¥76.982 million of net income and CN¥0.050831 of basic EPS over that period.
  • What is most important for a bearish angle is that even after adjusting for one off effects, the company still shows a five year pattern of earnings declining around 4.8% a year, and the latest half year loss of CN¥28.372 million on CN¥300.562 million of revenue reinforces the idea that volatility in profit is not just a one time event.
    • The CN¥6.049 million loss in 1H 2024 followed by CN¥105.354 million of net income in 2H 2024 and then a CN¥28.372 million loss in 1H 2025 gives a run of results that jumps between loss and profit rather than forming a steady climb.
